intro.kicker: Topic 5 of 10 • Chapter: Newton's Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Newton's Second Law states that the rate of change of linear momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and occurs in the direction of the force. The fundamental form is F = dp/dt; for constant mass this reduces to F = ma. This law gives the quantitative definition of force and is derivable to encompass the First and Third Laws. It is the most fundamental of Newton's three laws and the core engine of NEET Mechanics problems.

expectedQuestions.description: Most numerically tested topic in Newton's Laws. Questions span direct F = ma applications, impulse-momentum, variable mass problems, and FBD-based multi-body setups.

timeRequired.description: Dedicate time to F = dp/dt derivation, F = ma applications, impulse, and multi-body problems. Draw FBDs for every practice question.

difficulty.description: The formula is simple; real difficulty lies in identifying the correct system, resolving forces in multiple dimensions, and handling variable mass (e.g., rocket propulsion).

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics covers F = ma but may handle variable mass scenarios (F = dp/dt form) less rigorously. NEET tests both forms and the derivation linking them.

weightage.sectionTitle: Newton's Second Law — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Highest-yield topic in Newton's Laws chapter — 2–3 questions nearly every NEET cycle.
FBD-based multi-body problems (Atwood machine, connected blocks) use F = ma as the core equation.
Impulse (F·Δt = Δp) is a direct application tested every 2–3 years.

strategySteps.title: Master both forms of the law
strategySteps.description: F = dp/dt (general form, works for variable mass). F = ma (constant mass — derived form). Know when each applies.

strategySteps.title: Practice FBD-first approach
strategySteps.description: For every Newton's Second Law problem, draw a free body diagram first. Identify all forces, their directions, and apply ΣF = ma along each axis.

strategySteps.title: Connect to impulse and momentum
strategySteps.description: F·Δt = Δp = m(v − u). Impulse-momentum problems are direct extensions. Master the impulse approximation for short-duration forces.

cards.title: Newton's Second Law — Full Notes
cards.description: F = dp/dt derivation, F = ma applications, impulse, FBD strategy, and multi-body problem templates.

cards.title: FBD Practice Problems Pack
cards.description: 30 solved FBD-based Second Law problems including Atwood machine, inclined planes, and pulley systems.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Newton's Second Law of Motion

cards.title: The Fundamental Statement
cards.coreRevision: The rate of change of linear momentum of a body is directly proportional to the external force applied on the body, and this change takes place always in the direction of the applied force.
points: F = dp/dt (general form — works even when mass changes)
F = ma (derived when mass is constant)
Force and acceleration are in the same direction
cards.example: A 5 kg block accelerated at 3 m/s² requires a net force of F = 5 × 3 = 15 N in the direction of acceleration.

cards.title: Derivation: F = dp/dt to F = ma
cards.coreRevision: F = dp/dt = d(mv)/dt = m(dv/dt) = ma (when mass m is constant). The constant K = 1 in both SI and CGS units by definition of unit force.
points: SI unit of force: 1 Newton = 1 kg × 1 m/s²
cards.example: One Newton is defined as the force that produces 1 m/s² acceleration in a 1 kg mass. This is the defining choice that makes K = 1.

cards.title: Second Law as Most Fundamental
cards.coreRevision: Newton's Second Law is the most fundamental of the three laws because both the First and Third Laws can be derived from it.
points: First Law: set F = 0 in F = ma → a = 0 (constant velocity)
Second Law gives quantitative definition of force
Third Law derivable using isolated systems and momentum conservation
cards.example: If two interacting bodies form an isolated system, F_net = dp_total/dt = 0 (no external force), so F₁₂ = −F₂₁ — this is Newton's Third Law derived from the Second.

cards.title: Impulse-Momentum Theorem
cards.coreRevision: F·Δt = Δp = m(v − u). The impulse of a force equals the change in momentum. Valid for constant or average force over Δt.
points: Large force, small time ≈ impulsive force (bat hitting ball, explosion)
Area under F–t graph = impulse = change in momentum
Unit of impulse = N·s = kg·m/s (same as momentum unit)
cards.example: A cricket bat exerts 500 N on a 0.16 kg ball for 0.01 s. Impulse = 500 × 0.01 = 5 N·s. Ball gains momentum 5 kg·m/s, so velocity gain = 5/0.16 = 31.25 m/s.

curriculum.hintText: Key differences in Newton's Second Law treatment between NEET and US AP Physics.

gaps.title: F = dp/dt vs F = ma
gaps.description: US AP Physics uses F = ma as the primary form. NEET teaches F = dp/dt as the fundamental form and derives F = ma.
bullets: Know F = dp/dt handles variable mass (rocket propulsion, rain accumulation on trolley)
F = ma only valid for constant mass systems — explicitly required in NEET

gaps.title: Second Law as Most Fundamental
gaps.description: NEET emphasises that Newton's Second Law is the most fundamental because First and Third Laws derive from it.
bullets: KImportant MCQ: 'Which is most fundamental?' Answer: Second Law
First Law is a special case (F = 0) of the Second Law

questions.question: Newton's second law of motion gives:
questions.explanation: Newton's 1st Law gives qualitative definition of force; 2nd Law gives quantitative definition (F = dp/dt or F = ma — force is measurable as rate of change of momentum).

questions.question: A constant force acts on a body of mass 0.5 kg, initially at rest, and produces a velocity of 20 m/s in 5 seconds. Find the force.
Which of Newton's laws is most fundamental?
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law (F = dp/dt) is the most fundamental because both the First Law (F = 0 case) and Third Law can be derived from it. This is explicitly stated in NCERT.

questions.question: A net force of 12 N acts on a 3 kg body. What is the acceleration?
A 2 kg body initially at rest is acted upon by a force for 4 s, giving it a velocity of 20 m/s. The force is:
The rate of change of linear momentum of a body is equal to:
The SI unit of force (Newton) is equivalent to:

items.title: Learn both law forms
items.description: F = dp/dt (general, for any mass); F = ma (constant mass derivation). Know when each applies.

items.title: Second Law = quantitative force definition
items.description: First Law = qualitative. This distinction is a frequent NEET MCQ.

items.title: Second Law is most fundamental
items.description: First and Third Laws can be derived from Second Law. Confirm you can explain how.

items.title: Impulse-Momentum theorem
items.description: F·Δt = Δp. Area under F–t graph = impulse. Unit of impulse = N·s = kg·m/s.

items.title: FBD practice
items.description: Draw free body diagram for every multi-body force problem. Identify net force along each axis before applying F = ma.

items.question: Why is F = ma only valid for constant mass?
items.answer: The general Second Law is F = dp/dt = d(mv)/dt. When mass m is constant, this becomes m·(dv/dt) = ma. For systems where mass changes with time (rockets expelling gas, raindrops accumulating on a trolley), you must use F = dp/dt and account for the mass change term. F = ma without correction gives wrong answers in those cases.

items.question: How is the First Law a special case of the Second Law?
items.answer: If the net force F = 0, then from F = ma: a = F/m = 0/m = 0. Zero acceleration means constant velocity (which includes being at rest). This is precisely Newton's First Law. The Second Law thus contains the First Law as its F = 0 limit.

items.question: What is the difference between force and impulse?
items.answer: Force (F) is the rate of change of momentum — it is an instantaneous or sustained quantity measured in Newtons. Impulse (J = F·Δt) is the total change in momentum delivered by a force over a time interval, measured in N·s (= kg·m/s). Impulse is what you integrate under the force-time curve.

grounding_summary: Page 190 states Newton's Second Law: rate of change of linear momentum is directly proportional to external force and occurs in the direction of the applied force. It derives F = dp/dt and simplifies to F = ma for constant mass.
Page 190 confirms Newton's Second Law gives the quantitative definition of force, and that First and Third Laws can be derived from Second — making Second Law the most fundamental.
grounding_excerpt: The rate of change of linear momentum of a body is directly proportional to the external force applied on the body and this change takes place always in the direction of the applied force.
Newton's second law gives the quantitative definition of force.
Newton's first and third law can be derived from second law therefore second law is the most fundamental law out of three.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion (Chapter 4)
sources.why_used: Authoritative NCERT source for F = dp/dt derivation and the most-fundamental-law claim.

sources.title: Laws of Motion Important Questions for NEET 2025
sources.why_used: NEET exam enrichment — high-yield questions and common mistakes on Newton's Second Law.

review.notes: All content grounded in page 190. Exact NCERT wording for all three excerpts. No LaTeX in verbatim excerpts.
